name: Physical constants (SI · cgs · natural)
description: Fundamental constants with their values across unit systems — SI, Gaussian-cgs, and natural units — plus why some are set to 1 and why dimensionless constants don't change.
primer:
Fundamental constants take **different numerical values in different unit systems** — except dimensionless ones, which are pure numbers.

### Unit systems

- **SI** — base mechanical units metre, kilogram, second (m, kg, s); energy in joules (J).
- **Gaussian-cgs** — centimetre, gram, second (cm, g, s); energy in **ergs** ($1\\ \\text{J}=10^{7}\\ \\text{erg}$). It folds $4\\pi\\varepsilon_0$ away, so charge is in **esu (statcoulombs)**.
- **Natural units** (particle physics) — set $c=\\hbar=1$ (often $k_B=1$ too). Lengths, times, masses and energies then share dimensions, quoted in powers of energy (eV); masses become **eV/$c^2$** (or just eV).
- A **dimensionless constant** (e.g. $\\alpha$) is a pure number — *the same in every system*.

### Mechanics & quantum

- **Speed of light** $c = 2.998\\times10^{8}$ m/s $= 2.998\\times10^{10}$ cm/s; $c=1$ (natural).
- **Planck constant** $h = 6.626\\times10^{-34}$ J·s $= 6.626\\times10^{-27}$ erg·s; sets the quantum of action ($E=h\\nu$).
- **Reduced Planck** $\\hbar = h/2\\pi = 1.055\\times10^{-34}$ J·s; $\\hbar=1$ (natural); $\\hbar c\\approx 197.3$ MeV·fm.
- **Gravitational constant** $G = 6.674\\times10^{-11}\\ \\text{m}^3\\text{kg}^{-1}\\text{s}^{-2}$ ($=6.674\\times10^{-8}$ cgs); least precisely known.

### Thermodynamics

- **Boltzmann** $k_B = 1.381\\times10^{-23}$ J/K ($=1.381\\times10^{-16}$ erg/K); $k_B=1$ (natural).
- **Avogadro** $N_A = 6.022\\times10^{23}$ mol⁻¹ (a count, so unit-independent).
- **Molar gas constant** $R = N_A k_B = 8.314\\ \\text{J·mol}^{-1}\\text{K}^{-1}$ ($pV=nRT$).
- **Stefan–Boltzmann** $\\sigma = 5.670\\times10^{-8}\\ \\text{W·m}^{-2}\\text{K}^{-4}$; blackbody $j^\\star=\\sigma T^4$.

### Electromagnetism

- **Elementary charge** $e = 1.602\\times10^{-19}$ C (SI) $= 4.803\\times10^{-10}$ esu (Gaussian).
- **Vacuum permittivity** $\\varepsilon_0 = 8.854\\times10^{-12}$ F/m; appears as $1/4\\pi\\varepsilon_0$ in Coulomb's law; absorbed in Gaussian cgs.
- **Vacuum permeability** $\\mu_0 \\approx 1.257\\times10^{-6}\\ \\text{N·A}^{-2}$ ($\\approx 4\\pi\\times10^{-7}$); $c=1/\\sqrt{\\mu_0\\varepsilon_0}$.
- **Fine-structure constant** $\\alpha = e^2/(4\\pi\\varepsilon_0\\hbar c)\\approx 1/137 \\approx 7.297\\times10^{-3}$; **dimensionless**, sets EM strength.

### Atomic & particle

- **Electron mass** $m_e = 9.109\\times10^{-31}$ kg $= 9.109\\times10^{-28}$ g; $m_e c^2 = 0.511$ MeV.
- **Proton mass** $m_p = 1.673\\times10^{-27}$ kg $\\approx 938.3$ MeV/$c^2$ ($\\approx 1836\\,m_e$).
- **Atomic mass unit** $1\\ \\text{u} = 1.661\\times10^{-27}$ kg $\\approx 931.5$ MeV/$c^2$ ($=1/12$ of a $^{12}$C atom).
- **Bohr radius** $a_0 = 5.292\\times10^{-11}$ m $= 0.529$ Å; atomic length scale.
- **Electron-volt** $1\\ \\text{eV} = 1.602\\times10^{-19}$ J — natural energy unit at atomic/particle scales.

cards.term: What are 'natural units' (particle physics), and which constants become 1?
cards.summary: Set $c=\hbar=1$ (often $k_B=1$ too) so equations lose those factors.
cards.definition: A system where $c=\hbar=1$ (and usually $k_B=1$, sometimes $\varepsilon_0=1$). Lengths, times, masses and energies then share dimensions; quantities are quoted in powers of energy (eV). It strips $c$ and $\hbar$ out of formulas.
cards.note: Restore SI by reinserting factors of $c$ and $\hbar$ on dimensional grounds.

cards.term: [Units] A dimensionless constant like the fine-structure constant α has the same numerical value in SI, cgs and natural units.
cards.summary: True — pure numbers are unit-system independent.
cards.definition: True. $\alpha \approx 1/137$ is a pure number (a ratio of quantities with the same dimensions), so no choice of units changes it. Only dimensional constants get different numbers in different systems.

cards.term: SI vs cgs: the base mechanical units are metre/kilogram/second versus {{centimetre/gram/second}}.
cards.definition: SI uses m, kg, s; (Gaussian) cgs uses cm, g, s. Energy is the joule (SI) vs the erg (cgs), with $1\ \text{J}=10^{7}\ \text{erg}$. Electromagnetism differs more: Gaussian-cgs folds $4\pi\varepsilon_0$ away, so charge is in esu (statcoulombs).

cards.term: Speed of light $c$ — value in SI, cgs, and natural units?
cards.definition: SI: $c = 299{,}792{,}458 \approx 2.998\times10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$ (exact, it defines the metre). cgs: $2.998\times10^{10}\ \text{cm/s}$. Natural units: $c = 1$ (dimensionless).

cards.term: Planck constant $h$ — SI and cgs value?
cards.definition: SI: $h = 6.626\times10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$ (exact, it defines the kilogram). cgs: $6.626\times10^{-27}\ \text{erg·s}$. It sets the quantum scale of action.

cards.term: Reduced Planck constant $\hbar$ — value, and in natural units?
cards.definition: $\hbar = h/2\pi = 1.055\times10^{-34}\ \text{J·s} = 1.055\times10^{-27}\ \text{erg·s}$. In natural units $\hbar=1$. A handy form: $\hbar c \approx 197.3\ \text{MeV·fm}$.

cards.term: Gravitational constant $G$ — SI and cgs value?
cards.definition: SI: $G = 6.674\times10^{-11}\ \text{m}^3\,\text{kg}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-2}$. cgs: $6.674\times10^{-8}\ \text{cm}^3\,\text{g}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-2}$. The least precisely known fundamental constant.

cards.term: Boltzmann constant $k_B$ — value, and in natural units?
cards.definition: SI: $k_B = 1.381\times10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ (exact, it defines the kelvin). cgs: $1.381\times10^{-16}\ \text{erg/K}$. With $k_B=1$ (natural), temperature is measured in energy units.

cards.term: [Constants] Avogadro's number $N_A$ is:
cards.definition: Correct: A) $6.022\times10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$. It's a count per mole (exact, defining the mole), so it isn't unit-system dependent the way dimensional constants are.

cards.term: Molar gas constant $R$ — value and relation?
cards.definition: $R = N_A k_B = 8.314\ \text{J·mol}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-1}$ (≈ $1.987\ \text{cal·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$). It's just Boltzmann's constant per mole instead of per particle.

cards.term: Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma$ — value and use?
cards.definition: $\sigma = 5.670\times10^{-8}\ \text{W·m}^{-2}\,\text{K}^{-4}$. A blackbody radiates $j^\star=\sigma T^4$. Derivable from $k_B$, $\hbar$, $c$.

cards.term: Elementary charge $e$ — SI value, and the Gaussian-cgs form?
cards.definition: SI: $e = 1.602\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ (exact, defining the ampere). Gaussian-cgs: $e = 4.803\times10^{-10}\ \text{esu (statC)}$ — a different unit because cgs builds charge from mechanical units, with no separate ampere.
cards.note: This SI↔cgs charge mismatch is why electromagnetism formulas look different in the two systems.

cards.term: Vacuum permittivity $\varepsilon_0$ — SI value, and what happens to it in Gaussian cgs?
cards.definition: SI: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854\times10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$, appearing as $1/4\pi\varepsilon_0$ in Coulomb's law. Gaussian-cgs has no $\varepsilon_0$: Coulomb's law is just $q_1q_2/r^2$, so the constant is effectively absorbed into the definition of charge.

cards.term: Vacuum permeability $\mu_0$ — value and link to $c$?
cards.definition: $\mu_0 \approx 1.257\times10^{-6}\ \text{N·A}^{-2}$ (historically exactly $4\pi\times10^{-7}$). With $\varepsilon_0$ it fixes the speed of light: $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}$.

cards.term: Fine-structure constant $\alpha$ — value and why it's special?
cards.definition: $\alpha = \dfrac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\hbar c} \approx \dfrac{1}{137.036} \approx 7.297\times10^{-3}$. It's dimensionless — the same in every unit system — and sets the strength of the electromagnetic interaction.

cards.term: Electron mass $m_e$ — SI, cgs, and natural (energy) value?
cards.definition: SI: $m_e = 9.109\times10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$. cgs: $9.109\times10^{-28}\ \text{g}$. In natural/particle units, masses are given as energy: $m_e c^2 = 0.511\ \text{MeV}$, so $m_e = 0.511\ \text{MeV}/c^2$.

cards.term: Proton mass $m_p$ — value in kg and in MeV/$c^2$?
cards.definition: $m_p = 1.673\times10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$, i.e. $m_p c^2 = 938.3\ \text{MeV}$. It is about 1836 times the electron mass.

cards.term: Atomic mass unit $u$ — value in kg and MeV/$c^2$?
cards.definition: $1\ \text{u} = 1.661\times10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$, defined as $1/12$ the mass of a $^{12}\text{C}$ atom; equivalently $931.494\ \text{MeV}/c^2$. Equals $1\ \text{g/mol} \div N_A$.

cards.term: Bohr radius $a_0$ — value in SI and ångström?
cards.definition: $a_0 = 5.292\times10^{-11}\ \text{m} = 0.529\ \text{Å}$ — the most probable electron–proton distance in hydrogen's ground state, the natural length scale of atoms.

cards.term: The electron-volt as an energy: $1\ \text{eV} = $ {{1.602×10⁻¹⁹}} joules.
cards.definition: $1\ \text{eV} = 1.602\times10^{-19}\ \text{J}$ — the energy gained by one elementary charge across one volt. It's the natural energy unit at atomic/particle scales (keV, MeV, GeV).

cards.term: [Constants] In natural units a mass is most naturally expressed in:
cards.sub: A) eV/$c^2$ (or just eV, with $c=1$)    B) coulombs    C) kelvin    D) seconds
cards.definition: Correct: A) eV/$c^2$ (or just eV, with $c=1$). Because $c=\hbar=1$, mass, energy and momentum share units, so particle masses are quoted as energies — e.g. the electron at $0.511\ \text{MeV}$.
cards.note: 938 MeV for the proton, 125 GeV for the Higgs, etc.
